coredata.dc:title: Portable tri-color ratiometric fluorescence paper sensor for intelligent visual detection of dual-antibiotics and aluminium ion
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Kangling
Chen, Yu
Zhou, Qin
Wang, Xiangni
Wang, Ruoyan
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: The toxicological effect between co-existed antibiotics and metal ions was dangerous to the ecological environment and public health. However, the rapid quantification tools with convenience, accuracy and low cost for the detection of multiple targets were still challenging. Herein, a portable tri-color ratiometric fluorescence paper sensor was constructed by coupling of blue carbon dots and fluorescence imprinted polymer for down/up conversion simultaneous detection of tetracycline and sulfamethazine. Interestingly, the cascade detection of aluminum ion was also realized based on the individual detection system of tetracycline without the assistance of complex coupling reagents. The detection limits of smartphone method for the visual detection of tetracycline, sulfamethazine and aluminum ion were calculated as 0.014 μM, 0.004 μM and 0.019 μM, respectively. The portable fluorescence paper sensor was applied for the visual detection of tetracycline, sulfamethazine and aluminum ion in actual samples successfully with satisfactory recoveries. With the advantages of rapidness, low cost, and portability, the developed portable fluorescence paper sensor provided a new strategy for the visual real-time detection of multiple targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tri-color ratiometric fluorescence
Intelligent
Paper strip
Antibiotics
Aluminum ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of intelligent fluorescence paper sensor
Analytical performance for detection of TC and SMZ
Analytical performance for cascade detection of Al3+
Fluorescence paper strips with smartphone for detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010 ) and Graduate Research Innovation Project of Jishou University ( Jdy22057 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of b-CDs (A), g-CdTe QDs(B) and r-CdTe QDs (C). SEM images of ZIF-L (D), g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L (E), g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP (F) and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@NIP (G). TEM images of g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP (H).

Selectivity and interference experiments of D-CTCRFS (A) and U-CTCRFS (B) for the detection of TC (20 μM) with diverse interfering substances (25 μM). Selectivity and interference experiments of D-CTCRFS (C) and U-CTCRFS (D) for the cascade detection of Al3+ (20 μM) with diverse interfering substances (25 μM). Selectivity and interference experiments of D-CTCRFS (E) and U-CTCRFS (F) for the detection of SMZ (8 μM) with diverse interfering substances (25 μM).

(A), (C) and (E) Fluorescence response patterns of D-CTCRFS for the individual or simultaneous detection of TC and SMZ under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. (In the inset of (A) and (C): Color changes of D-CTCRFS for the detection of TC and SMZ). (B)and (D) Linear relation of fluorescence ratio values ((F547 + F646)/F440) of D-CTCRFS for individual detection of TC and SMZ. (F) Linear relation of fluorescence ratio values (F440/(F440)0) of D-CTCRFS toward TC concentrations. (G) Linear relation of fluorescence ratio values (F547+F646/(F547+F646)0) of D-CTCRFSFS toward SMZ concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence response patterns of TC@D-CTCRFS for the cascade detection of Al3+. (inset: Color changes of TC@D-CTCRFS for the detection of Al3+). (B) Linear relation of fluorescence ratio values ((F547 + F646)/F440) of TC@D-CTCRFS with Al3+ concentrations. (C) Fluorescence response patterns of TC@D-CTCRFS for the simultaneous detection of Al3+ and SMZ. (D) Linear relation of fluorescence ratio values (F440/(F440)0) of TC@D-CTCRFS toward Al3+. (E) Linear relation of fluorescence ratio values (F547+F646/(F547+F646)0) of TC@D-CTCRFS toward SMZ.

(A) Preparation of fluorescence paper strip and intelligent detection. (B), (E) and (H) Smartphone-assisted fluorescence paper strip coated with D/U-CTCRFS for the visual detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection of Al3+. (C) Linear relation of R + G/B values and TC (0.02–20 μM), Al3+ (0.02–20 μM) and SMZ (0.005–8 μM). (D), (G) and (J) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram of the fluorescence test strip responding to TC, Al3+and SMZ.
Stability of fluorescence color changes of fluorescence paper strips for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade detection Al3+ in chicken (A) and shrimp (B).

(A) Preparation of fluorescence materials and imprinted polymer. (B) D/U-CTCRFS for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade detection of Al3+ and smartphone with paper strip based on D/U-CTCRFS for the visual detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection of Al3+.

head.ce:title: Portable tri-color ratiometric fluorescence paper sensor for intelligent visual detection of dual-antibiotics and aluminium ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Hunan 416000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Changsha University, Changsha, 410022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biological and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Changsha University, Changsha, 410022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources Chemistry and Pharmacology in Wuling Mountainous of Hunan Province College, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources Chemistry and Pharmacology in Wuling Mountainous of Hunan Province College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Medicinal Resources Chemistry and Pharmacology in Wuling Mountainous of Hunan Province College, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The toxicological effect between co-existed antibiotics and metal ions was dangerous to the ecological environment and public health. However, the rapid quantification tools with convenience, accuracy and low cost for the detection of multiple targets were still challenging. Herein, a portable tri-color ratiometric fluorescence paper sensor was constructed by coupling of blue carbon dots and fluorescence imprinted polymer for down/up conversion simultaneous detection of tetracycline and sulfamethazine. Interestingly, the cascade detection of aluminum ion was also realized based on the individual detection system of tetracycline without the assistance of complex coupling reagents. The detection limits of smartphone method for the visual detection of tetracycline, sulfamethazine and aluminum ion were calculated as 0.014 μM, 0.004 μM and 0.019 μM, respectively. The portable fluorescence paper sensor was applied for the visual detection of tetracycline, sulfamethazine and aluminum ion in actual samples successfully with satisfactory recoveries. With the advantages of rapidness, low cost, and portability, the developed portable fluorescence paper sensor provided a new strategy for the visual real-time detection of multiple targets.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) and sulfamethazine (SMZ) as broad-spectrum antibiotics have been commonly used in animal husbandry and aquaculture to control various bacterial diseases [1,2]. However, the excess of TC and SMZ enriched in the human body through the food chain can result in serious toxic side effects (allergic reaction, gastrointestinal disturbance, hepatotoxicity kidney damage and cancer) [3–5]. Aluminum, as a common metal element, has been widely used in food additives, cosmetics, pharmaceutical synthesis and water treatment. However, human activities and environmental acidification can observably increase the amount of free aluminium ion (Al3+) in water, bodies of animals and human. Excessive of Al3+ in the human body can lead to Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer’s disease, renal osteodystrophy, and even breast cancer [6]. Meanwhile, the synergistic toxicological effect between co-existed antibiotics and metal ions may cause greater harm to the ecological environment and human health [7,8]. With the increasing concerns about water quality and public health, development of a simply, efficient, and reliable analytical method to detect antibiotics and metal ions in water environments and foods is highly demanded. Recently, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical analysis and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) have been widely used in the individual detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+ [9–11]. However, the applications of these methods were limited due to the high technical skills, complicated instrument, expensive devices and time-consuming sample pretreatments. Compared with other methods, fluorescence analysis method has attracted extensive attentions due to its simple operation, fast response time and low price [12–17].

Fluorescence materials involving CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs), metallic nanoclusters (MNC), fluorescent metal–organic frameworks (FMOFs) and carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used to detect ions and antibiotics [18–22]. However, the poor stability of MNC and FMOFs limited their applications in fluorescence analysis field. CDs and CdTe QDs have the unique characteristics of down/up-conversion fluorescence including good visualization, high fluorescence efficiency, high photochemical stability, large anti-stokes shift, narrow emission peak, and deep tissue penetration depth [23–26]. Fu et al. reported a dual channels fluorescence based on CDs@ZIF-8 for the detection of Fe3+ and glutathione [27]. Hu et al. designed a multiplex chroma response wearable hydrogel patch based on the up-conversion optical probe for visual monitoring of urea in body fluids[28]. This method proved that the down- and up-conversion fluorescence probe have high sensitivity and accuracy.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been widely used in sample pretreatment, chromatographic separation and chemical/biological sensing due to their high specific binding sites complementary to the template molecules in shape, size and functional groups [29–31]. Because of their excellent sensitivity and selectivity, fluorescence imprinted polymers have been extensively used in the monitoring of human health and food safety [32]. Fu et al. proposed a dual-response ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on metal–organic frameworks for ultrasensitive visual detection of 4-nitrophenol in environments [33]. Even with the above fluorescence method has a lower detection limit and larger detection range, it was still challenging for the naked eye to precisely identify the slight color changes of the fluorescence sensor.

Smartphones combined with paper strips as a quantitative detection tool have been applied for tracing various target species due to the portability, high-quality imaging mode and optimizing computing capabilities [34–36]. Xu et al. designed a smartphone-integrated fluorescence test paper for visual detection of tetracycline [37]. The previous reports indicated smartphones combined with paper strips can achieve real-time detection of target in complex environment. However, the simultaneous detection of antibiotics and aluminum ion based on the fluorescence sensor combined with smartphones reported rarely.

Herein, a down/up conversion tri-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor (D/U-CTCRFS) by coupling blue carbon dots (b-CDs) with green/red-CdTe QDs fluorescence imprinted polymer (g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP) was designed for visual detection of TC and SMZ. TC can only quench the fluorescence characteristic peak at 440 nm of b-CDs while two fluorescence characteristic peaks at 547 nm and 646 nm of g/r-CdTe QDs in MIPs can be quenched by SMZ. Interestingly, the TC@D/U-CTCRFS system for the cascade detection of Al3+ was designed by D/U-CTCRFS and TC solution, in which the characteristic fluorescence peak at 440 nm of b-CDs increased due to the complexation force between Al3+ and TC [38], while peaks of the characteristic fluorescence remained constant at 547 nm and 646 nm. More importantly, the color changes of the test strip coated with D/U-CTCRFS were recognized by a smartphone App (Color Max) for the visual detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+. The portable fluorescent paper based on D/U-CTCRFS provided a new method for the real-time visual detection of multi-target.
Experimental section was displayed in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of intelligent fluorescence paper sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Scheme 1A, b-CDs, g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP were synthesized respectively by hydrothermal method, self-assembly method and sol–gel method. The D/U-CTCRFS was designed by coupling of b-CDs and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP without the assistance of complex coupling reagents. The ratiometric fluorescence response of the D/U-CTCRFS relied on the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm, 547 nm and 646 nm. Based on previous literatures [39,40], the volume ratios of b-CDs and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP were optimized to construct a D/U-CTCRFS. As demonstrated in Fig. S1A-S1F, the responses of the D/U-CTCRFS prepared with three volume ratios of b-CDs and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP (1:8, 1:9, and 1:10) toward different concentrations of TC or SMZ were tested, in which the corresponding color changes were displayed in Fig. S1G. When the volume ratio of b-CDs and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP was 1:9, the fluorescence color of D/U-CTCRFS for the detection of TC were changed from offwhite to yellow to orange. Meanwhile, the fluorescence colors of D/U-CTCRFS for the detection of SMZ were changed from offwhite to purple to blue. However, the color range of D/U-CTCRFS with other two volume ratios (1:8 and 1:10) was not as plentiful as that with the volume ratio of 1:9. Thus, the volume ratio value (1:9) of b-CDs and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP was selected as the best condition to construct a down/up-conversion tri-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

As displayed in Scheme 1 B, under excitation wavelength of 370 nm and 700 nm, the D/U-CTCRFS was developed to detect TC, SMZ and cascade detection of Al3+. Meanwhile, the TC@D/U-CTCRFS for simultaneous detection of Al3+ and SMZ can achieve. Thus, this strategy achieved the individual or simutenous detection of multi-target in complex environments. Meanwhile, an optical paper strip intelligent platform based on D/U-CTCRFS was designed for the real-time visual detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection of Al3+. The fluorescence color changes of paper strips were identified and extracted by a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp, which then were converted RGB values. These results showed that the portable smartphone combined with a fluorescence paper strip achieved the real-time detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection of Al3+.

The morphology of b-CDs, g-CdTe QDs, r-CdTe QDs, ZIF-L, g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L, g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@NIP were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A- 1C, b-CDs, g-CdTe QDs, and r-CdTe QDs showed approximately circular shapes with uniform diameters in TEM images. Their average particle sizes were 6.1 nm, 3.2 nm, and 4.0 nm, respectively. The SEM images in Fig. 1 D showed that the ZIF-L with a leaf-shaped morphology has an average size of 1.0 μm, which was kept with previous literature [41], indicating that the ZIF-L was synthesized successfully. As shown in Fig. 1E, the surface of g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L was coarse with bulges compared with pure ZIF-L. This was attributed to the aggregates of g-CdTe QDs and r-CdTe QDs on the surface of ZIF-L [42]. As displayed in Fig. 1F and 1G, the spheric morphology of g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@NIP was similar with an average particle size of 1.4 μm, which was greater than that of the g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L (1.1 μm). Meanwhile, as displayed in Fig. 1H, the imprinted layer was coated on the surface of g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L, suggesting that the g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP was synthesized successfully. Additionally, the images of energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis, FT-IR analysis and XRD analysis of the all materials were displayed in Figure S2- S3 and the related discussions were recorded in the Supporting Information. All results showed that the b-CDs, g-CdTe QDs, r-CdTe QDs, ZIF-L, g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L, g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@MIP and g/r-CdTe QDs@ZIF-L@NIP were prepared successfully.

The discussion of excitation wavelengths, pH, response time and temperature of the D/U-CTCRFS during the detection of TC and SMZ were given in Supporting Information and the relevant images were presented in Figure S4. The results indicated that the optimal detection parameters for TC and SMZ detection were down-conversion excitation wavelength (370 nm), up-conversion excitation wavelength (700 nm), pH (7.0), response time (2 min) and temperature (30 °C).

In order to achieve the cascade detection of Al3+, some conditions including pH, response time and TC concentration were optimized. The results were discussed in Supporting Information and the corresponding pictures were shown in Figure S4. According to above results, the optimal detection settings for the cascade detection of Al3+ was TC concentration (20 μM), pH (7.0), and response time (2 min).

Additionally, in order to evaluate the selectivity of D/U-CTCRFS, the structural analogs of TC and SMZ (OTC, TAP, ENR, DA, SMZ, SDZ, SFD, SFZ) and metal ions including Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+ and Ag+ were selected to evaluate their interference during the detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+. The effect of analogs (25 μM) and metal ions (25 μM) for detecting TC with the D/U-CTCRFS were studied, and the results were displayed in Fig. 2 A and 2B. The effect of analogs and metal ions for fluorescence intensity ratio values ((F547 + F646)/F440) were slight except for Hg2+. Interestingly, the effect of Hg2+ for fluorescence intensity values (F547 + F646)/F440 of the D/U-CTCRFS can be eliminated by a masking agent (1.5 mol/L NH4OH). These results indicated that the D/U-CTCRFS has good selectivity for the detection of TC. As described in Fig. 2C and 2D, when Al3+ solution was added to the mixture solution of TC and D/U-CTCRFS (TC@D/U-CTCRFS), the fluorescence ratio values (F547 + F646)/F440 of the TC@ D/U-CTCRFS decreased observably, while other metal ions (25 μM) were slight influence except for Hg2+. Meanwhile, other metal ions (25 μM) hardly affect the TC@D/U-CTCRFS in the presence of Al3+ except for Hg2+, while the effect of Hg2+ for fluorescence intensity values (F547 + F646)/F440 of the TC@D/U-CTCRFS can be eliminated by a masking agent (1.5 mol/L NH4OH), which was indicated that the TC@D/U-CTCRFS has good selectivity for the detection of Al3+. As described in Fig. 2E and 2F, 1.5 mol/L of NH4OH masking agent was used to eliminate the interference of Hg2+. The D/U-CTCRFS exhibited higher selectivity toward SMZ (8 μM) than structural analogs (25 μM) and metal ions (25 μM), which was mainly due to the specific binding site of SMZ. All the results showed that the D/U-CTCRFS has anti-interference ability and good selectivity for detection of TC, SMZ and cascade detection of Al3+. In addition, the fluorescence color changes of selectivity and anti-interference test of fluorescence paper strip for detection of TC, SMZ and cascade detection of Al3+ were displayed in Figure S8 of the Supporting Information. These results show that the fluorescence paper strip based on D/U-CTCRFS exhibits excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence stability of D/U-CTCRFS solution was studied. As shown in Figure S9A and B, the fluorescence intensity ratio value ((F547 + F646)/F440) of D/U-CTCRFS solution kept stable for three months after the D/U-CTCRFS was preserved at room temperature, which indicated that the D/U-CTCRFS had good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for detection of TC and SMZ
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performances of D/U-CTCRFS were studied for individual detection of TC and SMZ under optimal conditions. Firstly, as displayed in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescent peak of D-CTCRFS at 440 nm was gradually quenched when the concentration of TC increased from 0.02 μM to 20 μM, while the fluorescence peaks remained constant at 646 and 547 nm. Meanwhile, the color changes image of D-CTCRFS for detection of TC was displayed in the inset of Fig. 3A, in which the fluorescence colors changed from offwhite to yellow to orange. Fig. 3B displays the linear relationship of fluorescence intensity ratio values ((F547 + F646)/F440) of the D-CTCRFS with TC concentrations ranged from 0.02 μM to 20 μM with a linear equation of Y = 0.121X + 2.714 (R2 = 0.997). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.005 μM based on 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of D-CTCRFS parallel-detecting 10 times (n = 10), S is the slope of standard curve.

The fluorescence quenching image of D-CTCRFS for the individual detection of SMZ was illustrated in Fig. 3C, in which SMZ concentrations increased from 0.005 μM to 8 μM, the fluorescence peaks at 547 nm and 646 nm were gradually quenched, while the fluorescence peak at 440 nm remained steady. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2C, the fluorescence color of D-CTCRFS for the detection of SMZ changed from offwhite to purple to blue. The excellent linear relationship of fluorescence intensity ratio values ((F547 + F646)/F440) of the D-CTCRFS and the different concentrations of SMZ (0.005 μM- 8 μM) was shown in Fig. 3D. The linear equation was Y = 2.721–0.217X (R2 = 0.996) and the LOD was calculated as 0.0014 μM based on 3S/N.

Moreover, the developed D-CTCRFS in this study was also capable of simultaneous detecting TC and SMZ. As can be seen in Fig. 3E, as TC concentrations increased from 0.02 μM to 20 μM, the intensity of the fluorescence peak at 433 nm of D-CTCRFS reduced gradually. Meanwhile, the two fluorescence peaks at 547 and 646 nm of D-CTCRFS were quenched gradually as SMZ concentration increased from 0.005 to 8 μM. Fig. 3F shows that an excellent linear relationship was existed between the fluorescence intensity ratio values (F440/F(440)0) of D-CTCRFS and TC concentrations (0.02 μM- 20 μM), in which a linear equation (Y = 0.962–0.038X) with the coefficients of R2 = 0.993 was obtained. Based on the results, a LOD of 0.0052 μM was calculated for the determination of TC. As shown in Fig. 3G, the fluorescence intensity ratio values ((F547 + F646)/(F547 + F646)0) of D-CTCRFS showed a good linear relationship in SMZ solution with different concentrations ranged from 0.005 μM to 8 μM with a linear equation of Y = 1.028–0.087X (R2 = 0.993). Thus, a LOD of 0.0018 μM was obtained for the determination of SMZ. All results showed that the D–CTCRFS for the individual or simultaneous detection of TC and SMZ were achieved successfully. Similarly, the up-conversion fluorescence analytical performance of the tri-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor (U-CTCRFS) for the detection of TC and SMZ were shown in Figure S5, in which the LOD of TC and SMZ was calcualted as 0.009 μM and 0.002 μM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for cascade detection of Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 A, the TC@D-CTCRFS for the cascade detection of Al3+ was achieved when Al3+ solution was added to the mixture system of D-CTCRFS containing TC at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The fluorescence peak at 440 nm increased gradually while the fluorescence peaks at 547 nm and 646 nm remained stable. Additionally, the color changes image of TC@D-CTCRFS for the detection of Al3+ was shown in the inset of Fig. 4A, in which the fluorescence color changed from yellow to buff to offwhite. Fig. 4B shows that the fluorescence efficiency of TC@D-CTCRFS which was evaluated with the fluorescence intensity ratio values of (F547 + F646)/F440 showed a good linear correlation with Al3+ concentrations ranged from 0.02 μM to 20 μM. The linear equation (Y = 5.208–0.121X) and coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.994) for detection of Al3+ were obtained. Based on the above results, a LOD of 0.01 μM was calculated for detecting of Al3+.

Similarly, the analytical performances of the TC@D-CTCRFS for the simultaneous detection of Al3+ and SMZ were studied. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, when the Al3+ solution was mixed with the TC@D-CTCRFS, the fluorescence peak at 440 nm increased gradually while the fluorescence peaks at 547 nm and 646 nm were quenched gradually. Fig. 4D shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio values (F440/F(440)0) of the TC@D-CTCRFS and Al3+ concentrations in the range of 0.02 μM- 20 μM have a good linear relationship with the corresponding linear equation of Y = 0.045X + 1.032 (R2 = 0.993). The LOD was calculated as 0.0092 μM. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence intensity ratio values ((F547 + F646)/(F547 + F646)0) of TC@D-CTCRFS and SMZ concentrations in the range of 0.005 μM- 8 μM has a good linear relationship, in which the linear equation and coefficients of determination (R2) were Y = 0.991–0.098X and 0.995, respectively. The LOD was calculated as 0.0016 μM. Similarly, the up-conversion analytical performance of the mixture solution of tri-color ratiometric fluorescence sensor and TC (TC@U-CTCRFS) for the detection of Al3+ was discussed in Figure S6, in which the LOD of Al3+ was calculated as 0.012 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence paper strips with smartphone for detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the detection capability of D/U-CTCRFS, a simple operation, low cost and portable fluorescence paper strips based intelligent sensor was developed to detect TC, SMZ and cascade detection of Al3+. The fluorescence paper strips intelligent sensor includes UV portable lamp, 3D dark box, smartphone and fluorescence test strips. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence test strips were prepared by coating D/U-CTCRFS directly on the filter paper, which exhibits faint offwhite fluorescence under the 3D dark box or a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating that the D/U-CTCRFS did not cause self-quenching in the filter paper. As seen from Fig. 5B, when different concentrations TC solutions (0.02 μM- 20 μM) were added to fluorescence paper strips, the fluorescence color of the fluorescence test strips changed from off-white to yellow to orange. Fig. 5C exhibits the excellent linear correlation between the R + G/B values and the concentrations TC solution within 0.02 μM-20 μM. Two linear equations were Y = 0.120X + 1.925 (R2 = 0.9991) and Y = 0.402X- 0.693 (R2 = 0.9993), respectively. The LOD was calculated as 0.014 μM. The CIE 1931 image displayed in Fig. 5D indicated that the color changes of image and fluorescence test strips changes for the detection of TC were consistent. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 5E, when different concentration Al3+ solutions were added the fluorescence test strips in the presence of TC solution, the fluorescence color of fluorescence test strips changed from yellow to buff to off-white under a 365 nm UV lamp. Fig. 5F displays that the R + G/B values of fluorescence paper strips and Al3+concentration have a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.02 μM- 13 μM and 13 μM- 20 μM. Two linear equations were Y = 8.816–0.512X (R2 = 0.993) and Y = 3.062–0.052X (R2 = 0.993), respectively. The LOD was calculated as 0.019 μM based on 3S/N. The CIE 1931 image displayed in Fig. 5G indicated that the color changes of image and fluorescence test strips changes for the detection of Al3+ were consistent. As seen from Fig. 5H, when different concentration SMZ solutions (0.005 μM −8 μM) were added to fluorescence test strips, which the fluorescence color of the fluorescence test strip changed from off-white to purple to blue. Fig. 5I exhibits that the excellent linear relationship was observed between the R + G/B ratio values and SMZ concentrations (0.005 μM- 3 μM and 3 μM- 8 μM), in which linear equations were Y = 2.042–0.133X (R2 = 0.995) and Y = 2.339–0.238X (R2 = 0.991), respectively. The LOD was calculated as 0.004 μM. The CIE 1931 image displayed in Fig. 5J indicated that the color changes of images and fluorescence test strip changes for the detection of SMZ were consistent. All results showed that the portable fluorescence paper sensor was achieved for rapid real-time intelligent detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+.

Lake water, shrimp, chicken, tea and urine were selected to discuss the practical application of D/U-CTCRFS for the visual detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+. Since TC, SMZ and Al3+ could not be to detect in the above practical samples, the recovery experiment was conducted to evaluate the feasibility of D/U-CTCRFS. Table S1 and S2 show that the fluorescence analysis method and smartphone analysis method for the detection of TC and SMZ have good recoveries. These results of this method were agreement with that of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis method. The spiked recoveries of the fluorescence method for the detection of TC and SMZ in lake water, shrimp, chicken, tea, and urine were calculated to be 96.0–104.0 % and 94.0–107.0 %, respectively. The spiked recoveries of smartphone method for the detection of TC and SMZ in lake water, shrimp, chicken, tea, and urine were calculated as 97.0–108.0 % and 96.0–110.0 %, respectively. Table S3 shows the fluorescence analysis method and smartphone analysis method for the cascade detection of Al3+ has good recoveries. The results were consistent with that of inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis method. The recoveries of the fluorescence method for the detection of Al3+ in lake water, fish, chicken and urine were calculated to be 98.0–108.0 %. The recoveries of the smartphone method for the detection of Al3+ in lake water, fish, chicken and urine were calculated to be 99.0–109.0 %. Meanwhile, the fluorescence paper strips detect the color change of the actual samples displayed in Table S1, Table S2 and Table S3. These results were consistent with HPLC and ICP-MS methods, indicating that the D/U-CTCRFS can serve as reliability sensors for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection Al3+. Additionally, the stability of fluorescence paper strips based on D/U-CTCRFS for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade detection Al3+ in actual samples (shrimp and chicken) was studied. As shown in Fig. 6 A and 6B, the colors of fluorescence paper strips kept stable within 7 days before and after adding TC, SMZ and Al3+, indicating fluorescence paper strip has good stability.

The performance comparison of D/U-CTCRFS and other fluorescence analytical methods in previous reports for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection of Al3+ were displayed respectively in Table S4, Table S5 and Table S6. The results showed that the D/U-CTCRFS for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade visual detection of Al3+ have a wider linear range and a lower LOD compared with most other methods. Furthermore, the D/U-CTCRFS can be used for the single or simultaneous detection of TC and SMZ or Al3+ in different environments. In addition, the smartphone-assisted paper strip analysis can achieve to real-time detect TC, SMZ and Al3+.

This work designed a sensitive D/U-CTCRFS by coupling of b-CDs and FMIPs for rapid visual detection of TC (0.02–20 µM), SMZ (0.005–8 µM) and cascade detection of Al3+ (0.02–20 µM). Meanwhile, a smartphone-assisted fluorescence paper strip achieved the real-time detection of TC, SMZ and Al3+ with good stability within 7 days. More importantly, the optical paper strip based on D/U-CTCRFS has been successfully applied for the detection of TC, SMZ and cascade detection of Al3+ in actual lake water, shrimp, chicken, tea, and urine samples with satisfactory recoveries. This work has potential application for the detection of multiple targets in the complex environment.

Kangling Tang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yu Chen: Resources. Qin Zhou: Data curation. Xiangni Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Ruoyan Wang: Validation. Zhaohui Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 22164010) and Graduate Research Innovation Project of Jishou University (Jdy22057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124221.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
